title: Area and perimeter word problems
description: Solve 1- and 2-step word problems involving area of rectangles and perimeter of polygons.
topic_path: Module 7: Geometry and measurement word problems
Topic D: Problem solving with perimeter and area

assessment_items.question_content:
Tom wants new carpeting for his bedroom. His room is a $9$ meters by $7$ meters rectangle. 

**How much carpeting does he need to buy to cover his entire bedroom floor?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Tom wants to find the amount of carpet it will take to **cover** his entire bedroom floor, so he is finding the **area**.
Tom needs $63$ square meters to cover his bedroom floor.

assessment_items.question_content:
Gina is getting a new bathroom mirror. The rectangular mirror measures $6$ meters by $8$ meters.

**What is the amount of space the mirror will cover on the wall?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Gina wants to find the amount of flat space the mirror will **cover** on the wall, so she is finding the **area**.
The mirror will cover $48$ square meters of the wall.

assessment_items.question_content:
Tomlin is removing the wallpaper. The wallpaper covers a $5$ meter by $4$ meter rectangular wall.

**How much wallpaper will Tomlin remove?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Tomlin wants to find the amount of wallpaper that **covers** his wall, so he is finding the **area**.
Tomlin will remove $20$ square meters of wallpaper.

assessment_items.question_content:
Gia is painting her wall. $1$ liter of paint can cover $10$ square meters. She has a rectangular wall that measures $4$ meters by $5$ meters. 

**How many liters of paint will Gia need to cover her wall?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Gia wants to find the amount of paint it will take to **cover** her entire wall, so she is finding the **area**.
Gia will need $2$ liters.

assessment_items.question_content:
Jose wants to cover his rectangular bathroom floor in tile. The floor measures $3$ units by $5$ units. Each tile is $1$ square unit and costs $\$4$.

**How much will it cost Jose to cover his entire bathroom floor in tile?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Jose wants to find the amount of tile it will take to **cover** his bathroom floor, so he is finding the **area**.

Now, Jose needs to find out how much it will cost to cover his $15$ square unit bathroom floor with tile.

If $1$ tile costs $\$4$, how much will it cost him to get $15$ tiles?
It will cost Jose $\$60$ to cover his entire bathroom floor.

assessment_items.question_content:
Sally is building a garden. She plans to build a rectangular garden measuring $6$ meters by $3$ meters. She wants to put a net over top of the garden to keep the birds out. She has $12$ square meters of netting in her garage.

**How much more netting will Sally need to cover her whole garden?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Sally wants to find the amount of netting it will take to **cover** her garden, so she is finding the **area**.

Now, Sally needs to find out how much more netting she will need if she already has $12$ square meters of netting.

If Sally needs to cover $18$ square meters but already has $12$ square meters, how much more netting will she need?
$18\text{ square meters} - 12\text{ square meters} = 6\text{ square meters}$
Sally needs $6$ square meters of netting.

assessment_items.question_content:
A restaurant has a rectangular patio section that is $8$ meters wide by $6$ meters long. They want to use fencing to enclose the patio.

**How much fencing will they need to go around their new patio?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] meters
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
The restaurant wants to find out the amount of fencing to go **around** the patio, so they want to find the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \greenD8\text{ m} +\purpleC6\text{ m}+ \greenD8\text{ m}+\purpleC6\text{ m}$
The restaurant will need $28\text{ m}$ of fencing to around their patio.

assessment_items.question_content:
Natalie runs around the outside of a square park in her city. Each side of the park measures $20$ meters. 

**How far did Natalie run around the park?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{ meters}$
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Natalie is going to run **around** the park, so she wants to find the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \greenD{20}\text{ m} +\purpleC{20}\text{ m}+ \greenD{20}\text{ m}+\purpleC{20}\text{ m}$
Natalie ran $80\text{ m}$ around the park.

assessment_items.question_content:
Christen has to run around the rectangular soccer field during practice. The soccer field measures $60$ meters long by $45$ meters wide.

**How far did Christen run?**

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] $\text{ meters}$
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Christian ran **around** the soccer field, so she ran around the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  


[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \greenD{60}\text{ m} +\purpleC{45}\text{ m}+ \greenD{60}\text{ m}+\purpleC{45}\text{ m}$
Christen ran $210\text{ m}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
A construction company is starting to build a new house. They need to put tape around the site to begin. The new home site is $20$ meters wide by $40$ meter long rectangle. The company already has $100$ meters of tape.  

**How much more tape does the company need to tape the site?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
The construction company wants to find out the amount of tape needed to go **around** the outside of the construction site, so they want to find the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \greenD{20}\text{ m} +\purpleC{40}\text{ m}+ \greenD{20}\text{ m}+\purpleC{40}\text{ m} = 120\text{ m}$

The construction company already has $100\text{ m}$ of tape but needs $120\text{ m}$. How much do they still need?

$120\text{ m} - 100\text{ m} = 20\text{ m}$
The construction company needs $20\text{ m}$ of tape.

assessment_items.question_content:
A rectangular football field measures $50$ meters by $25$ meters. The football team has to run around the field $4$ times during warm ups.

**How many meters does the team run during warm ups?**
  
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
The football team has to run **around** the outside of the football, so let's find the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \greenD{25}\text{ m} +\purpleC{50}\text{ m}+ \greenD{25}\text{ m}+\purpleC{50}\text{ m} = 150\text{ m}$

The team has to run around the field $4$ times.

$150\text{ m} + 150\text{ m}  + 150\text{ m}  + 150\text{ m} = 600\text{ m}$
The team runs $600\text{ m}$ during warm up.

assessment_items.question_content:
Steph needs to help her parents put a fence around their pool. They want the fence to be square and want each side to measure $6$ meters. They already have $10$ meters of fencing. 

**How many more meters of fencing should they buy?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
**Perimeter** is the distance around the outside of a two-dimensional shape.

**Area** is the space inside of a two-dimensional shape. We can also think of area as the amount of space a shape covers.
Steph is putting a fence **around** her parent's pool, so she needs to find the **perimeter**.

We can find the perimeter by adding all of the side lengths.  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\text{Perimeter} = \greenD{6}\text{ m} +\purpleC{6}\text{ m}+ \greenD{6}\text{ m}+\purpleC{6}\text{ m} = 24\text{ m}$

Her family already has $10$ meters of fencing. How much more fencing will they need?

$24\text{ m} -10\text{ m}= 14\text{ m}$
Steph's family needs $14$ meters of fencing.
